72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.

107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.

245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.

281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

4380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_image_opens_identities}
4381	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}
4382	  % NOTE (Lean): in the structure-sheaf restriction calculus these equalities read
4383	  %   (specBasicOpen g).ι ''ᵁ ((basicOpenIsoSpecAway g).inv ''ᵁ ⊤) = specBasicOpen g  and
4384	  %   (specBasicOpen g).ι ''ᵁ ((basicOpenIsoSpecAway g).inv ''ᵁ D(f̄)) = specBasicOpen(gf).
4385	  Let \(g, f \in R\), let \(R_g = R[g^{-1}]\) be the localisation of \(R\) away from \(g\), and
4386	  let \(\iota : \Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g) \hookrightarrow X\) be the open immersion
4387	  identifying \(\Spec R_g\) with the basic open \(D(g) \subseteq X = \Spec R\) (the composite of the
4388	  canonical homeomorphism \(\Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g)\) with the open inclusion). Then the image
4389	  opens of \(\iota\) of the two relevant opens of \(\Spec R_g\) are
4390	  \[
4391	    \iota\bigl(\Spec R_g\bigr) = D(g),
4392	    \qquad
4393	    \iota\bigl(D(\bar f)\bigr) = D(gf) = D(g) \cap D(f),
4394	  \]
4395	  where \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under the localisation map \(R \to R_g\).
4396	\end{lemma}
4397	\begin{proof}
4398	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}
4399	  \leanok
4400	  The canonical homeomorphism \(\Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g)\) carries \(\Spec R_g\)
4401	  isomorphically onto \(D(g)\), so the first identity is just the image of the whole space under that
4402	  homeomorphism followed by the open inclusion. For the second, \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under
4403	  \(R \to R_g\); its basic open \(D(\bar f) \subseteq \Spec R_g\) maps under the homeomorphism to
4404	  \(D(g) \cap D(f)\), since restricting to \(D(g)\) and then taking the locus where \(f\) is invertible
4405	  is the locus where both \(g\) and \(f\) are invertible. Finally \(D(g) \cap D(f) = D(gf)\) since the
4406	  basic open of a product is the intersection of the basic opens. These are equalities of opens, proved
4407	  set-theoretically; they are not definitional.
4408	\end{proof}

4412	  \label{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq}

4414	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module, let \(W \subseteq \Spec R\) be open, let

4417	  action of the restricted global-sections image of \(r\):
4418	  \[
4419	    r \cdot x \;=\; c_R \cdot x_{\mathcal{F}},
4420	    \qquad
4421	    c_R \;=\; \rho^{W}\bigl(\theta_R(r)\bigr),
4422	  \]
4423	  where \(\theta_R : R \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\Spec R, \mathcal{O})\) is the global-sections
4424	  identification, \(\rho^{W}\) is restriction of sections from \(\Spec R\) to \(W\), and \(x_{\mathcal{F}}\)
4425	  denotes \(x\) regarded as a section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(W\) for its structure-sheaf module
4426	  structure (the underlying carrier is unchanged).

4429	
4430	\leanok

4433	  identification \(\theta_R\). Unfolding that definition, the \(R\)-scalar \(r\) acts as the structure-sheaf
4434	  scalar \(\rho^{W}(\theta_R(r))\) on the unchanged underlying section; the equality therefore holds by
4435	  definitional unfolding.

4443	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module and let \(g \in R\). Write

4445	  restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\) along the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) followed by the

4448	  transports to the ambient structure-sheaf action of \(\mathcal{F}\):

4453	  the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) and by the open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow
4454	  \Spec R\), and \(m_{\mathcal{F}}\) denotes \(m\) regarded as a section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the

4459	  \uses{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
4460	  \leanok

4462	  the inverse of the open immersion's global-sections ring map. The tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) is the
4463	  composite of two such restrictions --- along the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\) and along
4464	  the open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\) --- so its action of \(c\) is, by definition, the
4465	  ambient \(\mathcal{F}\)-action of \(\alpha_g^{-1}(\beta_g^{-1}(c))\) on the unchanged underlying section.

4467	  iterated-image form \(W\) (rather than rewriting it to \(D(g)\)) is what makes the two carriers
4468	  coincide on the nose.

4502	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities, lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq,
4503	    lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq}
4504	  The two section modules \(\Gamma_{R_g}(V,\mathcal{F}_{(g)})\) and \(\Gamma_R(\iota(V),\mathcal{F})\)
4505	  share the same underlying carrier. Indeed, keeping the \(\mathcal{F}\)-side open in its
4506	  iterated-image form \(W = \iota(V)\) (rather than rewriting it to a basic open), the carriers coincide
4507	  definitionally by the restriction-of-objects identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}; the only
4508	  difference between the two sides is the bundled module packaging --- an \(R_g\)-module on the left versus
4509	  an \(R\)-module on the right --- which lives at the level of the ambient module category, not in the
4510	  carriers.

4513	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq} the native \(R\)-action of the global-ring section functor
4514	  \(\Gamma_R(-,\mathcal{F})\) is the structure-sheaf action of the restricted global-sections image; by
4515	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq} the \(R_g\)-action of the tile
4516	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(-,\mathcal{F}_{(g)})\) transports through the two open-immersion ring isomorphisms to the
4517	  same ambient \(\mathcal{F}\)-action on the same carrier. Thus the carrier identification of
4518	  Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib} is already additive and respects restriction maps and naturality in
4519	  \(V\); to make it the asserted \(R_g\)-linear isomorphism it remains only to reconcile the two scalar
4520	  prescriptions.
4521	
4522	  Comparing the two action formulas (and reducing to the case \(V = \top\), so that \(W = D(g)\) by
4523	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}), the sole remaining content is a single structure-sheaf ring
4524	  identity: for \(r \in R\),
4525	  \[
4526	    \rho^{D(g)}\bigl(\theta_R(r)\bigr)
4527	      \;=\;
4528	    \beta_g^{-1}\bigl(\theta_{R_g}(\bar r)\bigr),
4529	    \qquad \bar r \;=\; \text{image of } r \text{ in } R_g,
4530	  \]
4531	  where \(\theta_R : R \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\Spec R,\mathcal{O})\) and \(\theta_{R_g} : R_g
4532	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\Spec R_g,\mathcal{O})\) are the global-sections identifications,
4533	  \(\rho^{D(g)}\) is restriction of sections to \(D(g)\), and \(\beta_g\) is the ring isomorphism on global
4534	  sections induced by the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \cong D(g)\). This identity is exactly the
4535	  statement that \emph{the sections of \(\mathcal{O}\) over \(D(g)\) are the localisation \(R_g\),
4536	  compatibly with the algebra map \(R \to R_g\)}: the left-hand side restricts \(r\) to \(D(g)\), and the
4537	  right-hand side is the same element viewed through the affine identification of \(D(g)\) with
4538	  \(\Spec R_g\) applied to the localisation map.
4539	
4540	  There are two routes to this ring identity.
4541	  \emph{(A) Geometric.} The open inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\) induces the identity on
4542	  sections, so the composite of the two open-immersion ring isomorphisms is the global-sections map of the
4543	  localisation morphism \(\Spec R_g \to \Spec R\), which is \(\Spec\) of the algebra map \(R \to R_g\).
4544	  The identity then follows from the naturality of the \(\Spec\)-to-global-sections comparison
4545	  (\(\Gamma\)-\(\Spec\) naturality) applied to that algebra map.
4546	  \emph{(B) Algebraic.} Rewriting \(W = D(g)\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}, the ring
4547	  \(\Gamma(D(g),\mathcal{O})\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from \(g\); both sides of the displayed
4548	  identity are then \(R\)-algebra maps \(R \to R_g\) into this localisation, and they agree by uniqueness
4549	  of the localisation comparison map.
4550	  The residual is a single focused structure-sheaf sub-proof; the carrier and scalar bookkeeping above is
4551	  definitional.
4552	\end{proof}

4562	  and let \(g \in R\) be either a cover element \(g = g_j\) or an overlap product \(g = g_j g_k\) of the
4563	  finite presentation cover of Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover}, so that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) of
4564	  \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(\Spec R_g\) admits a global presentation. Then the restriction-of-sections map
4565	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits its target as the localisation of
4566	  its source at the powers of \(f\); equivalently, there is a canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism
4567	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4568	\end{lemma}
4569	\begin{proof}
4570	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen, lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation,
4571	    lem:restrict_obj_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap,
4572	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities, lem:tile_section_comparison}
4573	  % NOTE: the naive recipe "the section comparison is the restrict_obj rfl" is UNSOUND:
4574	  % restrict_obj is rfl only for the local-ring SheafOfModules functor Γ(M,-), whereas
4575	  % the localisation in this file lives in the global-ring functor (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj),
4576	  % which does NOT commute with restriction definitionally. Hence the five-step descent below.
4577	  Let \(\iota : \Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g) \hookrightarrow X\) be the open immersion of
4578	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}, with \(\bar f\) the image of \(f\) under the
4579	  localisation map \(R \to R_g\). The proof is a base-ring descent in five steps; the section
4580	  comparison it rests on is \emph{not} the definitional equality of
4581	  Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib} (see the proof of
4582	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}).
4583	
4584	  \emph{Step 1 (global presentation of the tile).} Since \(D(g)\) lies in a presentation member of the
4585	  cover, step B4 (Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}) presents the tile
4586	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) globally over \(\Spec R_g\).
4587	
4588	  \emph{Step 2 (localisation over \(R_g\)).} Apply
4589	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation} to the globally presented
4590	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) and \(\bar f\): the section-restriction map
4591	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(\Spec R_g, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}) \to \Gamma_{R_g}(D(\bar f), \mathcal{F}_{(g)})\)
4592	  exhibits its target as a localisation at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{as \(R_g\)-modules}. Note this
4593	  is over \(R_g\), not yet over \(R\).
4594	
4595	  \emph{Step 3 (opens identities).} By Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities} the two image opens of
4596	  \(\iota\) are \(\iota(\Spec R_g) = D(g)\) and \(\iota(D(\bar f)) = D(gf) = D(g) \cap D(f)\).
4597	
4598	  \emph{Step 4 (the section comparison).} By Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison} there is an
4599	  \(R_g\)-linear isomorphism, natural in \(V\) and intertwining restriction maps,
4600	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(V, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}) \cong \Gamma_R(\iota(V), \mathcal{F})\). This is the load-bearing
4601	  step: it bridges the global-ring section functor (where Step 2's localisation lives) and the ambient
4602	  sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(R\); it is a genuine natural-isomorphism construction, not a definitional
4603	  identification. Transporting Step 2's localisation-at-\(\bar f\) property across this isomorphism
4604	  (\(R_g\)-linearly) and matching source and target opens by Step 3 yields: the map
4605	  \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits its target as a
4606	  localisation at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{still as \(R_g\)-modules}.
4607	
4608	  \emph{Step 5 (base-ring descent to \(R\)).} Finally descend the base ring from \(R_g\) to \(R\) by
4609	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap}, applied with \(A = R_g\) and the
4610	  scalar tower \(R \to R_g\) on the two section modules: since \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under
4611	  \(R \to R_g\), the powers of \(\bar f\) descend to the powers of \(f\), and the \(R\)-linear
4612	  restriction of the Step-4 map exhibits \(\Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) as the localisation of
4613	  \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of \(f\) \emph{over \(R\)}. Equivalently there is a
4614	  canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4615	
4616	  Crucially this localisation is carried out entirely on the tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\), where
4617	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is associated to a module (the tile is globally presented, hence of the form
4618	  \(\widetilde{M_g}\)), and never on
4619	  the global object \(\mathcal{F}\). This is exactly the structural feature that keeps the keystone
4620	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule} non-circular: the keystone-at-\(g\) statement is
4621	  \emph{not} invoked; only the presented-tile lemma is.
4622	\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Open-immersion section iso reads as a restriction]
  \label{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an open immersion of schemes. On global sections \(f\) induces the comparison
  ring map \(f^{\sharp}_{\top} : \Gamma(Y, \mathcal{O}_Y) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\), and the
  open-immersion section isomorphism
  \(\beta_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma\bigl(f(\top), \mathcal{O}_Y\bigr)\)
  identifies the global sections of \(X\) with the sections of \(Y\) over the image open
  \(f(\top) \subseteq Y\). Then the composite of \(f^{\sharp}_{\top}\) with \(\beta_f^{-1}\) is the
  structure-sheaf restriction of \(Y\) from \(\top\) down to the image open:
  \[
    \beta_f^{-1} \circ f^{\sharp}_{\top} \;=\; \rho^{f(\top)}_{Y}.
  \]
  Equivalently, under the section identification \(\beta_f\) the global-sections comparison map of \(f\)
  is nothing but restriction along \(f(\top) \hookrightarrow Y\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  The forward map of \(\beta_f\) is, by construction, the comparison map \(f^{\sharp}\) read on the image
  open. Rewriting the identity in the form \(f^{\sharp}_{\top} = \rho^{f(\top)}_Y \circ \beta_f\) and
  applying the naturality of the comparison maps \(f^{\sharp}\) with respect to the restriction maps of
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and \(\mathcal{O}_Y\), the right-hand side reduces to \(f^{\sharp}_{\top}\)
  precomposed with the identity restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) on \(\top\); the source open \(\top\)
  and its image are matched by the thin-category uniqueness of open inclusions. This leaves exactly
  \(\rho^{f(\top)}_Y\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[$\Gamma$--$\Spec$ naturality of the localisation immersion, section form]
  \label{lem:key_morph}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.key_morph}
  \uses{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
  Let \(g \in R\), write \(R_g = R[g^{-1}]\), let \(\lambda : R \to R_g\) be the localisation map, and let
  \(\iota = \Spec(\lambda) : \Spec R_g \to \Spec R\) be the induced affine morphism (it is the open
  immersion identifying \(\Spec R_g\) with \(D(g) = \iota(\top)\)). Then the restriction to the image
  open \(D(g)\) of the global-sections identification
  \(\theta_R : R \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\Spec R, \mathcal{O})\) factors as the localisation map
  followed by the global-sections identification of \(\Spec R_g\) and the (inverse) section isomorphism
  of \(\iota\):
  \[
    \rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R
    \;=\;
    \beta_\iota^{-1} \circ \theta_{R_g} \circ \lambda,
  \]
  where \(\theta_{R_g} : R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\Spec R_g, \mathcal{O})\) is the global-sections
  identification of \(\Spec R_g\) and \(\beta_\iota\) is the open-immersion section isomorphism of
  \(\iota\) at \(\top\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res}
  This is the naturality of the \(\Spec\)-to-global-sections comparison applied to the ring map
  \(\lambda\). Concretely, the Mathlib naturality square for the global-sections identification (the
  \(\Gamma\)-\(\Spec\) adjunction unit, \texttt{ΓSpecIso} naturality) gives
  \(\lambda \) followed by \(\theta_{R_g}\) equals \(\theta_R\) followed by the comparison map
  \(\iota^{\sharp}_{\top}\) of \(\iota = \Spec(\lambda)\). Substituting this into the right-hand side and
  reading the composite of \(\iota^{\sharp}_{\top}\) with \(\beta_\iota^{-1}\) as the restriction to the
  image open \(D(g)\) by Lemma~\ref{lem:appTop_appIso_inv_eq_res} yields \(\rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Composition of the two tile section isomorphisms]
  \label{lem:tile_appIso_comp}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_appIso_comp}
  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:key_morph} the localisation immersion \(\iota\) factors as the
  composite of the affine identification \(\Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g)\) (of schemes) with the open
  inclusion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow \Spec R\). The (inverse) open-immersion section isomorphisms of these
  two factors compose into the single (inverse) section isomorphism \(\beta_\iota^{-1}\) of \(\iota\) at
  \(\top\), up to a structure-sheaf transport along the equality of image opens
  \(\iota(\top) = D(g)\):
  \[
    \beta_{\hookrightarrow}^{-1} \circ \beta_{\mathrm{aff}}^{-1}
    \;=\;
    \tau \circ \beta_\iota^{-1},
  \]
  where \(\beta_{\mathrm{aff}}\) and \(\beta_{\hookrightarrow}\) are the section isomorphisms of the
  affine identification and of the open inclusion respectively, and \(\tau\) is the structure-sheaf
  transport induced by the equality of the composed image open with \(D(g)\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  The functoriality of the open-immersion section isomorphism under composition of open immersions
  (\texttt{comp\_appIso}) expresses \(\beta_\iota\) as the composite of the two factor isomorphisms, with
  the section isomorphism of the open inclusion being the identity. Folding the resulting transport
  through the equality of image opens \(\iota(\top) = D(g)\) gives the displayed identity, with \(\tau\)
  the structure-sheaf map of that equality of opens.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Structure-sheaf ring identity of the tile comparison, morphism form]
  \label{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_section_ring_identity}
  \uses{lem:key_morph, lem:tile_appIso_comp}
  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:key_morph}, the restriction to the tile image open \(D(g)\) of the
  global-sections identification \(\theta_R\) of \(\Spec R\) equals the localisation map followed by the
  global-sections identification of \(\Spec R_g\) and the two (inverse) open-immersion section
  isomorphisms of the tile:
  \[
    \rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R
    \;=\;
    \beta_{\hookrightarrow}^{-1} \circ \beta_{\mathrm{aff}}^{-1} \circ \theta_{R_g} \circ \lambda.
  \]
  This is the morphism-level (ring-map) form of the residual ring identity of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:key_morph, lem:tile_appIso_comp}
  Substitute the \(\Gamma\)-\(\Spec\) naturality identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:key_morph}, which rewrites
  \(\rho^{D(g)} \circ \theta_R\) as \(\beta_\iota^{-1} \circ \theta_{R_g} \circ \lambda\), and then
  replace the single section isomorphism \(\beta_\iota^{-1}\) by the composite of the two tile factor
  isomorphisms using Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_appIso_comp}. The structure-sheaf transport \(\tau\) of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_appIso_comp} merges with the restriction maps in the thin category of opens
  (functoriality of the structure presheaf), and the two displayed sides coincide.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[Scalar compatibility of the tile comparison at the source open]
  \label{lem:tile_scalar_compat}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_scalar_compat}
  \uses{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq, lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq,
    lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}\)-module, let \(g, r \in R\), and let
  \(x\) be a section of the affine tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) over \(\top\) --- equivalently, regarding
  the underlying carrier, a section of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the tile image open \(D(g)\). Then the native
  \(R\)-action of \(r\) on \(x\) (through \(\Gamma_R(-,\mathcal{F})\)) coincides with the \(R_g\)-action
  of the localised scalar \(\lambda(r) \in R_g\) on \(x\) (through the tile
  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(-, \mathcal{F}_{(g)})\)):
  \[
    r \cdot x \;=\; \lambda(r) \cdot x,
    \qquad \lambda : R \to R_g \text{ the localisation map}.
  \]
  This is the scalar core, at the source open \(V = \top\), of the section comparison of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}; it is the scalar-tower compatibility \(R \to R_g\) on the
  common underlying section carrier. It is \emph{not} the full natural isomorphism, only the equality of
  the two scalar actions on the single open \(V = \top\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq, lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq,
    lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq} the native \(R\)-action of \(r\) on \(x\) is the
  structure-sheaf action of \(\rho^{D(g)}(\theta_R(r))\), and by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq} the \(R_g\)-action of \(\lambda(r)\) on \(x\) (as a
  tile section) transports to the structure-sheaf action of
  \(\beta_{\hookrightarrow}^{-1}(\beta_{\mathrm{aff}}^{-1}(\theta_{R_g}(\lambda(r))))\) on the same
  underlying carrier. Both actions therefore reduce to a structure-sheaf scalar action, and the two
  scalars agree by the morphism-level ring identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_ring_identity}
  evaluated at \(r\).
\end{proof}

The two section modules \(\Gamma_{R_g}(V,\mathcal{F}_{(g)})\) and \(\Gamma_R(\iota(V),\mathcal{F})\)
  share the same underlying section \emph{type}. Indeed, keeping the \(\mathcal{F}\)-side open in its

  coincide by the restriction-of-objects identity of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}. This is an
  equality of the underlying section \emph{types} only: the \emph{bundled} module objects do \emph{not}
  coincide definitionally. The tile side is an \(R_g\)-module object and the \(\mathcal{F}\) side an
  \(R\)-module object; these are different objects of different module categories
  (\(\mathrm{Mod}\,R_g\) versus \(\mathrm{Mod}\,R\)), not the same bundled object. So the comparison is a
  reconciliation between two distinct module structures carried by one and the same underlying type, not a
  bare definitional identification of bundled modules.

  The two scalar actions \emph{reduce} to a common form, but their reconciliation is not definitional. By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesSpecToSheaf_smul_eq} the native \(R\)-action of the global-ring section functor
  \(\Gamma_R(-,\mathcal{F})\) is the structure-sheaf action of the restricted global-sections image; by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:modulesRestrictBasicOpen_smul_eq} the \(R_g\)-action of the tile
  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(-,\mathcal{F}_{(g)})\) transports through the two open-immersion ring isomorphisms to a
  structure-sheaf action on the same underlying carrier. Both of these are the two \texttt{rfl} scalar
  bridges. After them, the additive carrier identification of Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib} already
